assembly_instructions: Tendon-driven, underactuated 3D-printed robotic hand from Yale's GRAB Lab. Four fingers arranged in a 2+2 pincer: two fingers on each side of the palm, all extending forward. Each finger is printed in Nylon 12 as three visible segments (proximal, middle, distal) separated by live flexure hinges — no pin joints. A single DC actuator inside the palm pulls four Spectra tendons through a central floating pulley tree so the whole hand closes in a conformal grasp when any tendon reaches the end of travel. A cylindrical wrist connector at the rear bolts the hand onto a robot arm; a cover plate closes the palm actuator bay. A small cylindrical object sits in the grasp zone to show the pincer closing around it.

info.japanese_name: イェール OpenHand モデルT
info.summary: Open-source, tendon-driven, underactuated four-fingered robotic hand from Yale's GRAB Lab. Single actuator drives all fingers through a floating pulley tree for adaptive grasping.
info.description: The Model T is part of the Yale OpenHand Project — a family of open-source, low-cost, 3D-printed robotic hands designed by Aaron Dollar's GRAB Lab at Yale. Model T uses a 2+2 pincer arrangement of four underactuated fingers; all four are driven from a single DC motor through a floating pulley tree that equalizes tendon tension. Because the pulley tree is mechanically floating, tendons equalize tension across the fingers — when one finger contacts an object, the others keep closing until they too make contact, producing a conformal grasp without any sensing. Finger joints are printed Nylon-12 flexure hinges rather than discrete pin joints, which drops the parts count and makes the whole hand robust to impact. All CAD, BOM, and build instructions are released under CC BY-NC 3.0 and have seeded dozens of academic variants.
info.operator: Yale GRAB Lab + the academic robotics community
info.contractor: Aaron Dollar / Yale GRAB Lab (OpenHand Project)
info.contract_date: OpenHand Project launched 2013; Model T widely built 2014 onward
info.contract_value: ≈US$500 in parts (one motor + printed shells + Spectra tendon)
info.status: Open-source, widely replicated in research labs

facts.label: Fingers
facts.value: 4 — 2+2 pincer, each 3 visible segments (proximal + middle + distal)

facts.label: Joints
facts.value: Printed Nylon-12 flexure hinges (no pin joints)

facts.label: Actuation
facts.value: 1 DC motor → floating pulley tree → 4 Spectra tendons

facts.label: Coupling
facts.value: Floating pulley tree equalizes tension across all four fingers

facts.label: Construction
facts.value: FDM/SLS 3D printing + off-the-shelf motor, Spectra line

parts.name: Palm / Base
parts.role: Printed Nylon-12 palm shell — houses the actuator, pulley tree, and tendon routing.

parts.name: Palm Cover Plate
parts.role: Printed cover that closes the top of the palm over the actuator bay.

parts.name: Wrist Connector
parts.role: Cylindrical adapter that bolts the hand to a robot-arm flange.

parts.name: Tendon Actuator (DC motor)
parts.role: Single geared DC motor + spool inside the palm — pulls all four tendons simultaneously.

parts.name: Floating Pulley Tree
parts.role: Free-floating two-stage pulley that equalizes tendon tension across the four fingers — the core of the underactuated mechanism.

parts.name: Finger 1 — Proximal
parts.role: Outer-left finger, proximal segment. Flexure hinge connects to the middle segment.

parts.name: Finger 1 — Distal
parts.role: Outer-left finger tip. Tendon anchors here.

parts.name: Tendon (Finger 1)
parts.role: Spectra tendon running along the underside of finger 1 from the pulley tree to the distal anchor.

parts.name: Grasped Object (sample)
parts.role: Small cylindrical object (≈32 mm diameter) centered in the grasp zone, surrounded by the four fingertips.
